design_philosophy.core_loop: Deploy cyborg agents to complete mission objectives (assassinations, persuasions, rescues) while managing team positioning, weapon selection, and resource economy through territorial taxation
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing aggressive territorial expansion and taxation revenue against the risk of civilian revolt and loss of conquered territories; managing limited squad size and agent durability across escalating threats
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Corporate overlord commanding elite cyborg operatives in a dystopian cyberpunk future, building a global empire through force and manipulation
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Unapologetically dark and violent presentation; narrative delivered entirely through manual rather than in-game storytelling; the game trusts players to understand moral ambiguity (playing as the villainous corporation); emphasis on tactical positioning in isometric real-time rather than turn-based strategy or action reflexes; economy-driven progression where military power is directly gated by taxation management

difficulty.lives_system: Squad-based: losing all agents results in game over; individual agents can be replaced by persuading civilians into the roster
difficulty.checkpointing: Mission-based structure; failure in a mission results in replay; over-taxation causing territorial revolt requires mission replay to recapture
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player controls taxation rate directly, creating risk/reward dynamic; player selects which agents to deploy and which cybernetic upgrades/weapons to prioritize, allowing for difficulty self-calibration through resource allocation strategy

progression.upgrade_system: Research and development tree for weaponry and cybernetic modifications (including agents' behavioral attributes); agents can be equipped with progressively more destructive weapons: pistols → Uzis → miniguns → flamethrowers → sniper rifles → time bombs → lasers → Gauss gun; special items like medikits, scanners, and Persuadertron
progression.unlock_mechanism: Funds earned through taxation of occupied territories; player must balance military spending against maintaining taxation at levels that prevent revolt
replayability_hooks: Territory revolt mechanic forces replaying missions if taxation strategy fails
Multiple rival syndicates (The Tao, Sphinx Inc., The Castrilos) with different tactical profiles
Customizable agent loadouts and cybernetic configurations allowing different tactical approaches to same mission
Expansion pack (Syndicate: American Revolt) provides additional campaign content

world_building_techniques: Backstory contained entirely in game manual rather than in-game exposition (cyberpunk setting with megacorps replacing governments, CHIP technology for population control)
Intro cutscene establishes agents as captured civilians who have been cybernetically enhanced and reprogrammed
Mission objectives and rival syndicate names provide minimal narrative context during gameplay
Environmental setting (isometric cityscape) reinforces dystopian corporate control aesthetic

notable_bosses: Atlantic Accelerator research station finale: wave-based encounter with multiple enemy agents rather than single boss; requires elimination of 'wave upon wave' of opposition

faction_indicators: Rival syndicates: The Tao, Sphinx Inc., The Castrilos
danger_signals: Territorial revolt when taxation exceeds acceptable levels
Loss of agents to enemy fire or mission failure
Rival syndicate military presence in contested territories
environmental_storytelling: Isometric cityscape displays player's territorial control (presumably through visual markers or color coding, though not explicitly detailed)
Mission locations represent global map progression (moving from territory to territory toward final Atlantic Accelerator station)
Increasing enemy agent presence and sophistication signals escalating conflict with rivals
